records.text: Still I'll choose Spotify. The recommendation algorithm of Spotify is unmatched. The song radio feature is unmatched too. Coming to the topic of "Hi Res", ur phone rarely have DACs that can take advantage of hish res music. So, don't waste your money on Hi res stuff unless you can actually set up an end to end his res system. The difference between Hi res and basic lossy music in these streaming services cannot be felt in almost all cases. Very few people have high res gear - even people who have high res gear, don't have hi res source, or a high res DAC. Every component from source to the earpiece has to be able to deliver and handle high res. So, for almost 99% (and more) the high res is just an expensive way to listen to the same sound. Not only this, the mastering of the record also has to be done to exploit high res gear. You cannot just listen to Kishore Kumar on a high res device and pretend that you're listening to something better. Finally comes the ability to perceive differences. Trained ears can tell the difference while sitting in an empty silent room. On the go, hardly anybody can. I've been listening to hifi equipments for over a decade.

records.text: Not gonna lie. I thought you are speaking absolute garbage and disregarded this and then went to research myself later, found youtube music sound way better and cleaner and found this explanation on reddit: I think what's happening is YouTube doesn't have normalization of all tracks. Spotify is -14 luffs I believe. Where if the master of the tracks isn't at this threshold then Spotify will bring up the loudness to match it. This killing some natural dynamic range which the mastering engineer had intended. This is what I reckon is the likely case? Songs on YouTube are streamed at their true dynamic range and not normalized. This would result in clearer and richer bass frequencies not being upward compressed and squashed to match every other song on the platform. Just changed to YouTube myself. As a pro live sound engineer I was using test tracks off Spotify and soon realized the same songs I used for testing PA systems have alot more clarity in the entire frequency spectrum.

usable_records.text: Still I'll choose Spotify. The recommendation algorithm of Spotify is unmatched. The song radio feature is unmatched too. Coming to the topic of "Hi Res", ur phone rarely have DACs that can take advantage of hish res music. So, don't waste your money on Hi res stuff unless you can actually set up an end to end his res system. The difference between Hi res and basic lossy music in these streaming services cannot be felt in almost all cases. Very few people have high res gear - even people who have high res gear, don't have hi res source, or a high res DAC. Every component from source to the earpiece has to be able to deliver and handle high res. So, for almost 99% (and more) the high res is just an expensive way to listen to the same sound. Not only this, the mastering of the record also has to be done to exploit high res gear. You cannot just listen to Kishore Kumar on a high res device and pretend that you're listening to something better. Finally comes the ability to perceive differences. Trained ears can tell the difference while sitting in an empty silent room. On the go, hardly anybody can. I've been listening to hifi equipments for over a decade.

usable_records.text: Not gonna lie. I thought you are speaking absolute garbage and disregarded this and then went to research myself later, found youtube music sound way better and cleaner and found this explanation on reddit: I think what's happening is YouTube doesn't have normalization of all tracks. Spotify is -14 luffs I believe. Where if the master of the tracks isn't at this threshold then Spotify will bring up the loudness to match it. This killing some natural dynamic range which the mastering engineer had intended. This is what I reckon is the likely case? Songs on YouTube are streamed at their true dynamic range and not normalized. This would result in clearer and richer bass frequencies not being upward compressed and squashed to match every other song on the platform. Just changed to YouTube myself. As a pro live sound engineer I was using test tracks off Spotify and soon realized the same songs I used for testing PA systems have alot more clarity in the entire frequency spectrum.
